ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for packaged Chinese herbal tea (liang cha). FOODON_03301099 (herbal tea beverage) is the closest defensible parent class. Packaged/RTD form is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_description: Herbal infusions (tea-type beverages from herbs other than Camellia sinensis)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LQ covers herbal infusions broadly. No specific code for packaged Chinese liang cha exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. This is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept specifically for packaged Chinese herbal tea identified. SNOMED CT does not routinely code commercial beverage variants at this specificity.

main_ingredients: herbal extracts (e.g. chrysanthemum, honeysuckle, licorice root, or similar traditional herbs)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one can or bottle of approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g, providing approximately 87.5 kcal and 21.5 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Herbal Tea (Packaged) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged Chinese herbal tea (cr_id D05010010). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical source with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened herbal/fruit tea beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged Chinese herbal tea (liang cha) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on analogous sugar-sweetened herbal and fruit tea beverages with similar sugar content (~9 g/100 ml) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (52 × 21.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 11.2 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (8.95 g/100 ml) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via fructose-mediated mechanisms, despite very low sodium (3 mg/100 ml).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this sugar-sweetened beverage, as regular consumption of added sugar at this concentration (≥5 g/100 ml) is independently associated with elevated blood pressure per AHA 2018 guidelines; choose the unsweetened or no-sugar variant instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.95 g added sugar per 100 ml and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar raises postprandial glucose rapidly and is classified as unsuitable for type 2 diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this packaged herbal tea as it is a sugar-sweetened beverage with approximately 9 g of rapidly absorbed sugar per 100 ml and no fibre to attenuate glycaemic response; select the no-added-sugar variant or unsweetened herbal tea instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages at this sugar concentration is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular risk, independent of sodium content.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit intake of this sugar-sweetened packaged herbal tea, as habitual SSB consumption (≥1 serving/day) is linked to a 20% higher CVD risk per meta-analyses; the no-sugar variant is preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (8.95 g/100 ml) with zero dietary fibre and no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; herbal extracts at typical packaged beverage concentrations do not provide clinically significant anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should choose the no-added-sugar variant or switch to whole herbal preparations (fresh ginger tea, turmeric tea, green tea) that provide genuine anti-inflammatory polyphenols without the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese herbal tea (packaged), known in Chinese as 凉茶 (liáng chá, literally 'cooling tea'), is a ready-to-drink traditional beverage commercially produced and sold in cans or bottles across Singapore, China, Hong Kong, and the broader Chinese diaspora. It is brewed from a blend of traditional Chinese medicinal herbs — commonly including chrysanthemum (菊花), honeysuckle (金银花), licorice root (甘草), and other cooling herbs — then sweetened with sugar and packaged for convenience. The flavour is mildly bitter, floral, and slightly sweet, with a light amber colour and thin, watery texture. Culturally, liang cha is consumed as a 'cooling' remedy in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) to reduce internal heat (上火, shàng huǒ), relieve sore throats, and support general wellness, particularly during hot weather or after consuming 'heaty' foods. Nutritionally, each 100 ml provides approximately 35 kcal, 8.6 g carbohydrate (of which 8.95 g is sugar), negligible protein (0.01 g), and zero fat and dietary fibre. Sodium is very low at 3 mg/100 ml. The beverage is low in calories but is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its added sugar content. It is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or those managing blood glucose, and its regular consumption is associated with elevated cardiovascular and hypertension risk despite low sodium. The no-added-sugar variant is the preferred alternative for health-conscious consumers. This product is widely available in Singapore supermarkets, convenience stores, and hawker centres.
common_user_queries: Is Chinese herbal tea good for diabetes?
How many calories in a can of Chinese herbal tea?
Is packaged herbal tea suitable for high blood pressure?
What is liang cha made of?
Is bottled Chinese herbal tea sugar-free?
Can I drink herbal tea if I have hypertension?
What is the sugar content of packaged Chinese herbal tea?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010010) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is a well-defined commercially packaged product with a canonical HPB entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar sugar-sweetened herbal beverages, which reduces GI confidence to medium; however, overall record confidence remains high due to the exact HPB nutrient match.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero by the HPB source for this packaged beverage formulation; this is a water-based herbal extract with no fat-containing ingredients. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as the beverage is a filtered liquid extract with no intact plant fibre. Protein_g is 0.01 g (trace), not zero. Saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero consistent with a plant-based beverage with zero fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this packaged Chinese herbal tea are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (D05010010) as of 2026. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened herbal and fruit tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for liang cha was found. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a beverage widely consumed in Singapore. Sugar content in input record shows 8.95 g/100g (per_100g.sugar) while health_suitability notes reference 9.0 g — the HPB value of 8.95 g is used as the authoritative figure throughout this record.
